burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery (also called North Cemetery), West Hartford CT. Gravestone: "HENRY BRACE & ABIGAIL his wife... She died Aug.07, 1802. EA. 5_." SOURCE: Hale Collection.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker name preserved as middle name across generations
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker married Henry Brace, and the Hooker surname survived as a middle name through her children, grandchildren, and beyond — a deliberate preservation through collateral lines. Examples include her son Edmond Hooker Brace, her grandson Hezekiah Hooker Brace, and her grandchildren Abigail Hooker Pelton and Edmund Hooker Pelton through her daughter Abigail Hooker Brace Pelton. The pattern preserves Hooker identity across family branches even as primary surnames change.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Two children married into one Deming sibling pair
narrative_blocks.body: Lory Sr's son Thomas Brace married Electa Alling Deming in 1823, and his daughter Eliza Ann Brace married Walter Deming in 1827. Electa and Walter were brother-and-sister, both children of Theron and Electa Ensign Deming of West Hartford. Two Brace siblings married two Deming siblings within four years — a classic small-town Connecticut close-network pattern.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut farmer of a Brace family line

narrative_blocks.header: His son outlived the war by weeks
narrative_blocks.body: His boy Orrin lived to see the Confederacy fall — then died that same spring of 1865, barely forty, weeks after the guns went silent. The other children scattered into Barkhamsted harness-making and hill-country farming.

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County CT (per Findagrave/WikiTree).

bio.bio_blurb: West Hartford choir leader and town treasurer

narrative_blocks.header: Treasurer of West Hartford for forty-six years
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Brace led the West Hartford Congregational Church choir for nearly half a century and served as treasurer of the Congregational society for 46 years. He was also treasurer of the Middle School District (42 years), Sunday school treasurer + librarian (37 years), Home Missionary Society treasurer (24 years), and Town Treasurer of West Hartford (18 years). He taught singing schools in West Hartford's different schoolhouses for many years.
He married Electa Alling Deming; his sister Eliza married her brother Walter — both Demings children of the same West Hartford parents. Two Brace siblings, two Deming siblings, one double knot.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught singing in the schoolhouses
narrative_blocks.body: For years he carried singing schools through the different schoolhouses of West Hartford, training the voices of a town whose Congregational choir he led for nearly fifty years.
Lucinda died in 1814 at the age of fifteen. Her parents gave the name to a sister born one year later.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother Abigail Hooker Brace
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Hooker Brace (b. 1774) carried both her mother's given name and her mother's maiden name Hooker — a deliberate naming tribute to the line.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore twin daughters Cynthia and Sylvia
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail and her husband Seth Pelton had twin daughters born May 23, 1793 in Litchfield New York: Cynthia and Sylvia. Both grew to adulthood. Sylvia married Nathan Beardsley; Cynthia is starred in EH (sub-entry has further family detail not yet enumerated in our records).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his first four children died young
narrative_blocks.body: Manning Brace and Lucy Webster lost three of their first four children: Mahala (b.1796) died age 5 in 1802; their first Lucy (b. Jan 1798) lived only 30 days; their first Fanny (b.1799) died age 5 in 1804. The fourth, Lucy Mahala (b.1801), lived to 1865 — given a name combining her two lost sisters' names.

parents.father_research_notes: Henry and Ann (Collier) Brace. SOURCE: Brace Genealogy.

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Brace rose from Lieutenant (1748) to Captain (1760) in the 4th Company of the First Regiment of Hartford's Colonial Militia. When his son Henry III marched toward New York in August 1776, Henry Sr. was 63 — too old for service under the militia's age limit of 50.

narrative_blocks.header: His will was witnessed by Noah Webster
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Brace's will was signed December 28, 1781 and proved September 27, 1787, sworn before Noah Webster, Justice of the Peace in Hartford. Webster was 29 and not yet famous; he published his first American speller the following year.
